source.title: Resistance Training Prescription for Muscle Function, Hypertrophy, and Physical Performance in Healthy Adults: An Overview of Reviews

evidenceBase.methodology: Umbrella review using the GRADE (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation) framework

coreMessage: The most meaningful gains come from a simple shift: moving from no resistance training to any form of resistance training. Training all major muscle groups at least twice a week matters far more than chasing the perfect prescription.

trainingGoals.name: Strength
trainingGoals.definition: How much weight a person can lift
keyPrinciples: Perform key/primary lifts at the beginning of the session
Training to absolute failure is not required

trainingGoals.name: Hypertrophy (Muscle Growth)
trainingGoals.definition: Increasing the size and volume of muscle

percentOf1RM.note: Broad loading spectrum is effective provided each set is taken close to failure

keyPrinciples: Total weekly set volume is the primary driver of muscle growth
Eccentric overload (the lowering/negative phase) is favorable for hypertrophy
Training to absolute failure is not required; stopping at 2-3 reps in reserve produces comparable outcomes with less fatigue

trainingGoals.name: Power
trainingGoals.definition: How fast a person can move a given load
keyPrinciples: Move the load as fast as possible during the concentric phase
Can be trained safely with simple movements: fast sit-to-stands, step-ups, medicine ball throws, jump variations, kettlebell swings

trainingGoals.name: General Health / Functional Movement
trainingGoals.definition: Improving everyday function such as gait speed and balance
keyPrinciples: Use fast lifting technique ("Power RT") rather than heavy loading
Particularly relevant for aging adults: muscular power declines faster than strength, often starting in the 4th-5th decade of life

debunkedMyths.name: The Fatigue Myth
debunkedMyths.oldBelief: Training must be taken to muscular failure to maximize strength or size gains
debunkedMyths.currentEvidence: Training to absolute failure is not necessary for strength or hypertrophy outcomes. Stopping 2-3 reps short of failure produces similar results with less fatigue and lower injury risk.

debunkedMyths.name: The Fancy Gym/Equipment Myth
debunkedMyths.oldBelief: Different equipment types (machines vs free weights) and increasing exercise complexity/variety are necessary for progress
debunkedMyths.currentEvidence: For most healthy adults, equipment type and exercise complexity do not significantly impact results.

debunkedMyths.name: The Beginners vs Advanced Myth
debunkedMyths.oldBelief: Beginners and advanced trainees require fundamentally different exercises and program structures
debunkedMyths.currentEvidence: Basic/foundational exercises are effective for everyone; training experience has little impact on the effectiveness of a given exercise.

progressiveOverloadMethods: Increasing movement speed or power output

notes: Exercises performed earlier in a session tend to show greater improvement; prioritize what matters most early in the workout
Consistency and adherence matter more than precise program optimization
Resistance training benefits extend beyond muscle: healthspan, longevity, metabolic function, injury prevention, and long-term functional independence
